name: arch_inv_allows_empty_layers
code:
proof fn phi_1_arch_inv_allows_empty_layers(arch: Arch)
    requires
        arch.inv(),
    ensures
        arch.layers.len() == 0,
{
}
reason: If inv() is satisfiable with zero layers, the architecture is vacuously valid but maps no address space — likely a degenerate case the spec should exclude.

name: arch_inv_forces_equal_entry_sizes
code:
proof fn phi_2_arch_inv_forces_equal_entry_sizes(arch: Arch)
    requires
        arch.inv(),
        arch.layers.len() >= 2,
    ensures
        arch.entry_size(0) == arch.entry_size(1),
{
}
reason: If inv() forced adjacent layers to have equal entry sizes, the hierarchical size decomposition would be broken — each layer should cover a strictly larger region than the next.

name: entry_base_collision_distinct_indices
code:
proof fn phi_3_entry_base_collision_distinct_indices(arch: Arch, layer: nat, base: nat, i: nat, j: nat)
    requires
        arch.inv(),
        layer < arch.layers.len(),
        i < arch.num_entries(layer),
        j < arch.num_entries(layer),
        i != j,
    ensures
        arch.entry_base(layer, base, i) == arch.entry_base(layer, base, j),
{
}
reason: If two distinct entry indices produced the same base address, page table entries would alias and the MMU mapping would be ambiguous.

name: well_formed_forces_layer_zero
code:
proof fn phi_4_well_formed_forces_layer_zero(dir: Directory)
    requires
        dir.well_formed(),
    ensures
        dir.layer == 0,
{
}
reason: If well_formed() could only be satisfied at layer 0, subdirectories at deeper layers could never be well-formed, breaking the recursive directory invariant.

name: inv_allows_zero_size_page_frame
code:
proof fn phi_5_inv_allows_zero_size_page_frame(dir: Directory)
    requires
        dir.inv(),
        dir.entries.len() > 0,
        dir.entries[0int] is Page,
    ensures
        dir.entries[0int]->Page_0.frame.size == 0nat,
{
}
reason: If a directory satisfying inv() could contain a page with a zero-size frame, it would represent an empty memory mapping — violating the expectation that every mapped page covers a positive region.
